shower.thought: # 35 Original Shower Thoughts
You measure your life in years, but you experience it in moments—and you can never go back to find the ones that mattered most.
Every time you remember something, you're not retrieving the original memory—you're remembering the last time you remembered it, like a photocopy of a photocopy fading with each generation.
The only time you've ever truly been alone is in your own mind, and even then, you're arguing with yourself.
Money is just collective agreement that something has value, yet we treat it like it's real.
We spend the first part of our lives learning how to be alive, and the rest of it learning how to accept we're dying.
Your skeleton is the only part of you that will outlast your ego.
The fact that we need alarm clocks means we've engineered a life so unnatural that our bodies refuse to wake up for it.
You've never actually seen your face—only reflections and photographs of it. You're the only person who can't verify what you actually look like.
Most of your personality is just how you've learned to cope with being you.
The reason parents seem irrational to teenagers is that teenagers are only seeing the present moment, while parents are living in a future the teenagers haven't reached yet.
You're nostalgic for versions of yourself that were probably suffering at the time.
Silence isn't the absence of sound—it's when sound stops bothering you enough to notice.
If you're uncomfortable in a conversation, the other person probably is too, but you're both too uncomfortable to mention the discomfort.
Your phone knows more accurate information about you than you do about yourself.
The worst part about growing old isn't the body deteriorating—it's realizing how much smaller the world became when you stopped being curious about it.
Every skill you have is just pattern recognition you've automated so well that it feels like instinct.
We call it "falling" asleep, but you're actually climbing into a parallel universe every night that your waking mind can't access.
The people you can't stand often make you hate things about yourself that you already knew were there.
You'll never know if the color red looks the same in your mind as it does in someone else's, so everyone might be living in slightly different universes.

motivational.thought: Progress is not measured by the distance traveled, but by the decision to keep walking. — Unknown
The obstacle you're afraid to face is often the gateway to your next version. — Unknown
Your inconsistency today is someone else's consistency tomorrow. Don't let that person be you. — Unknown
Comfort is the enemy of growth, but growth is the friend of purpose. — Unknown
Every expert was once uncertain. The difference is they chose uncertainty over stagnation. — Ralph Waldo Emerson
The person who complains about their circumstances is living in the past. The person who acts is building the future. — Unknown
Discipline is choosing between what you want now and what you want most. — Unknown
You are only one decision away from a completely different life. Make it today. — Unknown
The things that scare you most are often the things that matter most. — Unknown
Momentum is not something you find—it's something you create by taking the first step, then the next, then the next. — Unknown
Your potential is infinite, but your time is finite. Spend it accordingly. — Unknown
The gap between who you are and who you want to be is not luck—it's habits. — Unknown
Regret is the price of playing it safe. Failure is the tuition for growth. — Unknown
What if the only thing holding you back is the story you keep telling yourself? — Unknown
You don't rise to the level of your goals; you fall to the level of your systems. — Unknown
The future does not belong to the talented. It belongs to the tenacious. — Unknown
Every moment is a fresh start, but most people don't take it. You're not most people. — Unknown
Your scars are not evidence of your weakness—they're proof of your survival. — Unknown
The difference between those who achieve and those who dream is simply this: those who achieve kept going when it got hard. — Unknown
Comparison is the thief of satisfaction, but it's the teacher of ambition. Use it wisely. — Unknown

darkjoke.thought:
I told my dad he'd be a great role model if he ever came back.
He said, "I am back," and left again.

My psychiatrist asked if I hear voices.
I said, "Only when people are talking to me."
She wrote something down and raised her rates.

I'm at that age where my back goes out more than I do.
Last week my spine threw a better party than I could.

My friend said suicide jokes aren't funny.
I said, "You're right, I'll kill that bit."

I told my cat she had 9 lives.
She seemed unimpressed by the redundancy.

Dating in your 30s is like job hunting—everyone wants experience, but nobody wants to train you.
Except the jobs are also disappointed in you.

My therapist asked what my goals are for next year.
I said, "Keep the streak alive."

I used to be addicted to soap, but I'm clean now.
The withdrawal was terrible though—I couldn't stop confessing things.
My gym membership is like my relationship status: inactive with no plans to change it.

People keep telling me I'm too negative.
I don't know what they mean—I thought I was being realistic.

I asked my doctor if I'd live to be 100.
He said, "Not at the rate you're going."
I said, "So, yes?"

My friend got a promotion at work.
I told him congratulations on his new anxiety level.

I have a stepladder because my real ladder left when I was just a kid.
We don't talk much.

My therapist says I'm self-sabotaging.
I said, "That's ridiculous," then immediately proved her right.

I told my girlfriend she was living in a fantasy world.
She said, "At least I'm happy there."
Fair point.

My dog is a better listener than my therapist.
Also cheaper, but he's less likely to write you a prescription.

I said to my accountant, "I'm worried I'm not saving enough for retirement."
He said, "At your current rate, retirement is the least of your concerns."

My ex said she needed space.
I said, "Great, take the whole apartment."
Turns out I was supposed to be the one leaving.

I started a new diet where I only eat when I'm actually hungry.
My therapist called it "concerning."

I told my boss I was thinking about leaving.
He said, "Please do—you're bringing the whole vibe down."
I was talking about for lunch.
